text: The surgeon mobilizes the greater curvature of the stomach from the gastric mid-body to the angle of His. The gastro-splenic ligament is divided using a vessel sealer device, effectively preventing bleeding from the short gastric vessels.
The surgeon divides the posterior fundal attachments to fully mobilize the gastric fundus, separating the attachments between the angle of His and the left crus.
The surgeon identifies the plane between the cruse and the esophagus, then addresses the right cruse by dividing the gastrohepatic ligament towards it.

The surgeon identifies and develops the plane between the esophagus and the right cruse, revealing the right vagus nerve coursing from the right lateral side and deviating posteriorly along the esophagus. Dissection is performed carefully to avoid nerve injury.

The surgeon develops the plane between the right cruse and the esophagus, dividing the phrenoesophageal membrane. Further dissection clearly defines the anatomical structures of the stomach, esophagus, and cruse.

The left vagus nerve is identified coursing anteriorly. Fat obscuring the lesser curve of the anterior stomach is removed, revealing the anatomy for fundoplication. An ethabond suture is placed to mark the gastroesophageal junction. The surgeon divides mediastinal areolar tissue circumferentially, mobilizing the esophagus to achieve an adequate intraabdominal length of three to four centimeters.

The surgeon performs a cruroplasty, bringing the left and right crura together posteriorly with a plugeted ethobond suture to recreate the hiatus. The fundus is wrapped posteriorly around the esophagus, with the left vagus nerve visible coursing anteriorly. The shoe shine maneuver is executed to symmetrically position the fundus.
The surgeon places three sutures to secure the fundus to the left side of the esophagus, ensuring that the left side of the fundoplication covers and protects the left vagus nerve.

The surgeon uses a sliding slip knot technique to secure sutures, starting with a square knot. The needle end of the suture is elevated straight up, and a slight tug on the exiting leg of the suture flips the knot into a sliding orientation. The slip knot is pushed down tightly and locked by pulling the two free ends of the suture in opposite directions.

The surgeon secures the right side of the fundoplication to the esophagus using three interrupted ethabond sutures. Two clips are placed on the proximal sutures to mark the cephalad aspect of the wrap, aiding in future identification on x-ray images.
